text: The surgeon begins the procedure by creating a peritoneal flap, starting laterally where a significant amount of adipose tissue is visible.

The surgeon visually assesses the three D max mid extra large mesh, determining the size of the pocket needed for placement during the robotic right femoral hernia repair.
The surgeon visually assesses the size of the three D max mid extra large mesh to determine the appropriate size of the pocket needed for placement.
The surgeon makes a lateral peritoneal incision, with the presence of fat allowing for precise dissection.
The surgeon makes a lateral incision in the peritoneum, with visible fat aiding in the precision of the incision.
The surgeon identifies two potential dissection planes and selects the plane adjacent to the peritoneum, preparing to dissect between the peritoneum and the testicular vessels.
The surgeon identifies two potential dissection planes and selects the plane adjacent to the peritoneum, preparing to dissect between the peritoneum and the testicular vessels.
The surgeon moves medially, carefully pushing the surrounding fat away to clear the surgical field.
The surgeon moves medially, pushing away the surrounding fat to clear the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the attenuated linea semilunaris, preparing to find the preperitoneal and retrorectus planes. The preperitoneal plane is preferred, although the peritoneum appears extremely thin in this patient.

The surgeon identifies the attenuated linea semilunaris, preparing to locate the preperitoneal and retrorectus planes. The preference is to remain in the preperitoneal plane, noting the peritoneum's thinness in very thin patients.
The surgeon pulls on the attenuated posterior rectus sheath to enter the retrorectus space, aiming to create a more robust peritoneal flap.
The surgeon pulls on the attenuated posterior rectus sheath to access the retrorectus space, creating a more robust peritoneal flap.

The surgeon is in the retro-rectus space, identifiable by the presence of abundant fat and the visibility of the inferior gastric vessel. The peritoneal flap is being pulled, revealing two distinct planes, with the surgeon opting to remain in the posterior rectus space to ensure a thicker peritoneal flap.

The surgeon is in the retrorectus space, identifiable by the presence of abundant fat and the visibility of the inferior gastric vessel. The peritoneal flap is being pulled, revealing two distinct planes, with the surgeon opting to remain in the posterior rectus space to ensure a thicker peritoneal flap.

The surgeon manipulates the tissue laterally, elevating the fat to reveal two distinct anatomical spaces: the space of Retzius and the space of Bogros. The peritoneum appears very thin in the space of Bogros, while a thicker posterior flap is visible in the space of Retzius.
The surgeon elevates the fat laterally, revealing two distinct anatomical spaces: the space of Retzius and the space of Bogros. The peritoneum in the space of Bogros appears very thin.

In the space of Retzius, the surgeon manipulates a thicker posterior flap, highlighting the transition between the Bagros and Retzius spaces. The attenuated posterior rectus sheath is divided at the linea semilunaris.
The surgeon demonstrates the transition between the Bagros space and the Retzius space, dividing the attenuated posterior rectus sheath at the attenuated linea semilunaris.
The surgeon observes the surgical planes in a patient with a higher BMI, noting that the planes are unusually thin despite expectations for more robust tissue layers.
The surgeon observes the surgical planes in a patient with a higher BMI, noting that the planes appear very thin despite expectations for more robust tissue layers.

The surgeon uses robotic precision to maintain the integrity of the fascial planes and peritoneum, with the left hand gently retracting the peritoneum while the right hand pushes away. Care is taken to avoid overstretching and damaging the thin peritoneum.

The surgeon uses robotic precision to maintain the integrity of the fascial planes and peritoneum, with the left hand gently retracting while the right hand pushes away, revealing a thin peritoneum layer.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the peritoneum with the left hand to avoid overstretching and potential tearing. The procedure progresses into the space of Retzius, where lobular fat is pushed up and kept against the rectus muscle, maintaining work in the least vascular plane. The surgeon then moves laterally, preferring a broad working area rather than a confined space.

The surgeon navigates deep into the space of Retzius, pushing lobular fat upwards and maintaining it against the rectus muscle, which is the least vascular plane. The surgeon then moves laterally, expanding the working area to avoid confined spaces.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the peritoneal flap to prevent damage and overstretching while taking down the peritoneum. The absence of an indirect inguinal hernia is visually confirmed during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the peritoneal flap to prevent damage and overstretching while taking down the peritoneum. The absence of an indirect inguinal hernia is visually confirmed during the procedure.

The surgeon uses the left hand for retraction while the right hand performs quick buzzing with a monopolar cautery to maintain hemostasis and safely dissect tissue. The dissection progresses deeper into the pelvis, revealing areolar tissue. Visible dimpling is noted from a previous open inguinal hernia repair, but no direct hernia is observed.

The surgeon uses left hand retraction to expose the pelvic area, while the right hand performs quick buzzing with a monopolar cautery to maintain hemostasis and safely dissect tissue. The retraction reveals areolar tissue, and dimpling is visible from a previous open inguinal hernia repair, though no direct hernia is observed.
The surgeon carefully navigates between the peritoneum and the testicular vessels, with the testicular artery visibly identified adjacent to the peritoneum.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the peritoneum and the testicular vessels, with the testicular artery visible. The focus is on maintaining hemostasis to clearly visualize and separate the peritoneum from posterior structures, minimizing cautery use and avoiding blood staining.

The surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum from the posterior structures, ensuring good hemostasis to maintain clear visibility of anatomical structures, while minimizing the use of cautery to avoid excessive blood staining.

The surgeon carefully stays on the peritoneum, using minimal cautery and gentle pushing to navigate the area. A small gap is visible, likely representing the peritoneum rather than the space of Retzius.

The surgeon carefully stays on the peritoneum, using minimal cautery and gentle pushing to maintain the integrity of the tissue. A small gap is visible, likely representing the peritoneum rather than an open space.
The surgeon identifies and attempts to reduce a femoral hernia in the male patient. The fascial layer enveloping the hernia is incised to facilitate reduction.
The surgeon identifies and attempts to reduce a femoral hernia in the male patient, incising the fascial layer enveloping the hernia to facilitate reduction.
The surgeon identifies the femoral hernia sac and applies gentle traction to release its contents, which gradually emerge from the sac.

The surgeon identifies the femoral hernia sac and applies gentle traction to release its contents. The surgical field is maintained with good hemostasis to prevent injury to the numerous vessels present in the area, minimizing the risk of significant bleeding.

The surgeon maintains good hemostasis while dissecting near numerous vessels to prevent bleeding, staying close to the peritoneum. The dissection progresses laterally and then more posteriorly before moving medially, loosening the flap to facilitate easier dissection of medial structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the peritoneum, moving laterally to create a loose flap before proceeding medially. This technique minimizes the risk of damaging critical structures such as the testicular vessels, vas deferens, iliac artery and vein, and the branch of the genitofemoral nerve overlying the iliac artery.

The surgeon navigates into the pelvic region, carefully avoiding damage to the testicular vessels, vas deferens, iliac artery and vein, and the branch of the genitofemoral nerve overlying the iliac artery.

The surgeon navigates into the pelvis, carefully using cautery near the iliac vessels due to the proximity to the genital nerve in a thin patient. The surgeon approaches the midline to locate the pubis, identifying a vessel crossing Cooper's ligament and extending posteriorly to the rectus muscle.

The surgeon carefully uses cautery near the iliac vessels, staying close to the genital nerve due to the patient's thin anatomy. The approach moves towards the midline to locate the pubis, identifying a vessel crossing Cooper's ligament and ascending posterior to the rectus muscle.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to divide tissue, ensuring hemostasis. The surgical field shows the area near the midline, preparing to cross it to ensure proper medial overlap of the mesh.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to divide tissue, ensuring the mesh overlaps medially as they prepare to cross the midline.

The surgeon identifies the linea alba where the two bellies of the rectus muscle meet in the midline. The dissection proceeds past the midline near the pubis, removing fat from the pubis while maintaining hemostasis due to small bleeding vessels. Mesh from a previous open inguinal hernia repair with a Prolene hernia system is visible.

The surgeon identifies the linea alba where the two bellies of the rectus muscle meet at the midline. Dissection proceeds past the midline near the pubis, removing fat from the pubic area while maintaining hemostasis due to the presence of small bleeding vessels. Mesh from a previous open inguinal hernia repair with a Prolene hernia system is visible.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum medially, revealing the iliac artery and pushing away surrounding structures near the vas deferens. Retraction towards nine o'clock aids in identifying the correct anatomical plane, as the surgeon approaches the obturator canal.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum off medially, revealing the iliac artery and pushing away structures surrounding the vas deferens. Retraction towards nine o'clock aids in identifying the correct anatomical plane, as the surgeon approaches the obturator canal.

The surgeon approaches the obturator canal, with the obturator nerve nearby. Cautery is applied in very short bursts to minimize risk. The obturator fat plug is visible, and instead of removing it, the surgeon divides the plug, leaving it within the obturator space.

The surgeon carefully uses cautery in short bursts near the obturator nerve, revealing the obturator fat plug. Instead of removing the fat plug, it is divided and left within the obturator space, with the surgeon aiming to access deeper areas.

The surgeon positions the mesh over the obturator internus and levator muscles, ensuring it lays deep into the pelvis to effectively cover the femoral space for the femoral hernia repair. The robotic visualization provides precise dissection in the pelvic region.

The surgeon positions the mesh over the obturator internus and levator muscles, ensuring it lays deep into the pelvis to effectively cover the femoral space, addressing the femoral hernia. The robotic system provides precise visualization for this pelvic dissection.

The surgeon carefully peels back the peritoneum to ensure a smooth transition from the pelvic space into the Bagros space, preventing any peritoneum from being trapped behind the mesh, which could lead to hernia recurrence.

The surgeon carefully peels back the peritoneum to ensure a smooth transition from the pelvic space into the Bagros space, preventing any peritoneum from being trapped behind the mesh, which could lead to hernia recurrence.

The surgeon navigates a challenging area prone to frequent bleeding, focusing on maintaining hemostasis to safely dissect the bloodied tissue. The obturator vein is visible, with the obturator nerve located posterior to it. The iliac vein is also identified in the surgical field.

The surgeon navigates a challenging area prone to frequent bleeding, focusing on maintaining hemostasis to safely dissect the tissue. The obturator vein is visible, with the obturator nerve located posterior to it. The iliac vein is also identified in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully peels the peritoneum, removing small blood vessels while preserving the fatty layer over the psoas muscle. The vas deferens and deferential artery are visible, and the medial umbilical ligament is identified, inserting into the iliac vein, distinct from the ureter.

The surgeon carefully peels off small blood vessels while maintaining the fatty layer over the psoas muscle. The vas deferens and deferential artery are visible, and the medial umbilical ligament is identified, inserting into the iliac vein, ensuring it is not confused with the ureter.

The surgeon carefully uses cautery near the obturator nerve to create additional posterior lateral space for mesh placement. The mesh is positioned to tuck into a pocket in the posterior lateral corner.

The surgeon carefully dissects near the obturator nerve, using cautery sparingly to create additional posterior lateral space for mesh placement. The mesh is positioned to tuck into a pocket in the posterior lateral corner.

The surgeon points out the femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve near the posterior lateral corner of the surgical field, highlighting the area where the mesh placement is challenging.

The surgeon points out the femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve near the posterior lateral corner, highlighting the area where the mesh placement is challenging.
The surgeon identifies the femoral and genital branches of the genitofemoral nerve over the psoas muscle while discussing the use of Progrip and 3D Max mesh.

The surgeon identifies the femoral and genital branches of the genitofemoral nerve over the psoas muscle. They search for a cord lipoma and observe a small amount of fat posterior to the iliopubic tract, identifying it as a lymph node and deciding not to touch it. The surgeon then identifies a potential lipoma and proceeds to remove it.

The surgeon examines the surgical field for a cord lipoma, identifying a small amount of fat posterior to the iliopubic tract. A lymph node is noted but left untouched. The surgeon decides to remove the lipoma, which may have contributed to the patient's reducible hernia and pain.
The surgeon identifies a small amount of fat that may have contributed to the patient's pain and decides to remove it, noting the patient's thin physique and the potential reducibility of the hernia.

The surgeon peels back the fascial plane over the nerves, staying close to the fat. The fascial plane is lifted over the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, and a small portion is removed through the port.

The surgeon peels back the fascial plane over the nerves, staying close to the fat layer. The fascial plane is lifted over the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, and a small portion is removed through the port.

The surgeon places the 3D Max mid extra large mesh on the right side, aligning the vertical line over the iliac artery. A small cord lipoma remains partially within the body, intended for later removal. The mesh is positioned slightly more medial, near the vas deferens.

The surgeon positions the 3D Max mid extra large mesh on the right side, aligning the vertical line over the iliac artery. A small cord lipoma remains partially within the body, intended for later removal.

The surgeon places a mesh that conforms well to the pelvic wall, suitable for femoral hernia repair. A stitch is being placed to Cooper's ligament to secure the mesh, ensuring it maintains posterior coverage of at least two to three centimeters.

The surgeon demonstrates how the mesh conforms to the pelvic wall, particularly suitable for a femoral hernia repair. A stitch is placed to Cooper's ligament to secure the mesh, ensuring it maintains posterior coverage of at least two to three centimeters.
The surgeon ties the stitches loosely to allow the mesh to adjust and conform to the pelvic pocket as desufflation occurs, ensuring proper positioning within the surgical site.

The surgeon ties the stitches loosely to allow the mesh to conform to the pelvic pocket upon desufflation. The lateral, anterior lateral edge of the triangle of pain is identified, and the mesh is sewn to ensure the posterior lateral edge remains down, preventing counterclockwise rotation and lifting.

The surgeon points out the anterior lateral edge of the triangle of pain and proceeds to sew the mesh, ensuring the posterior lateral edge remains secured to prevent counterclockwise rotation and lifting.

The surgeon adjusts the mesh by pushing it down in a clockwise direction before inserting the needle. The mesh appears to be positioned well, eliminating the need for a medial stitch at the conjoint tendon.

The surgeon adjusts the mesh by pushing it down in a clockwise direction before inserting the needle. The mesh is positioned well, eliminating the need for a medial stitch at the conjoint tendon. A standard closure is performed using a 3 V-lock suture, measuring 186 inches.
The surgeon performs a standard closure using a 3 V-lock suture, carefully manipulating the thin perineum and lower flap to avoid creating a hole due to excessive upward pressure.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the lower flap in the perineum, which is visibly thin, to avoid creating a hole. Despite being in the retroactive space, the posterior flap layer remains thin, requiring cautious handling.

In the retroactive space, the surgeon manipulates the thin posterior flap layer by running it back with two throws, pulling it taut and releasing before cutting it. A small hole is visible, which the surgeon decides not to close, aiming to desufflate the extra peritoneal space to ensure the mesh lays flat.

The surgeon runs the suture back two throws, pulling it taut and then releasing before cutting it when not taut. A small hole is visible, which the surgeon decides to close to ensure proper desufflation of the extraperitoneal space, allowing the mesh to lay flat.

The surgeon demonstrates tying a knot carefully on the thin peritoneum to avoid ripping and enlarging the existing hole. The knot is laid squarely without pulling the suture too much, ensuring gentle placement to prevent tension-related damage.
The surgeon demonstrates tying a knot on the thin peritoneum, carefully laying it square without pulling too much on the suture to avoid enlarging the hole under tension.

The surgeon uses a 16-gauge angiocath inserted by the fellow at the bedside, allowing the pneumo peritoneum to push the peritoneum up without the need for aspiration. The posterior edge of the mesh is laid down smoothly.
